nativeRange.habitat: The McCullochi Dottyback inhabits holes and crevices in coral and rocky reefs across tide pools, lagoonal reefs, and reef slopes, at depths reported from around 5 to 40 m. Individuals in the wild are generally observed alone or in pairs and tend to be cryptic, keeping close to shelter.

diet.notes: Generally regarded as a meaty-food carnivore. Has been reported to readily accept vitamin-enriched mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped marine seafood, and quality marine flake or pellet preparations. Once-daily feeding is typically advised, with multiple smaller feedings sometimes recommended for newly introduced specimens.

avoidWith: fish of similar shape or color

imageCandidates.notes: Debra Baker; 1527x1148. Vibrant pink-orange adult in full side profile, whole fish in frame against dark background; clearly shows the oblique blue-purple head lines, fine dotted body pattern and green eye ring diagnostic of Cypho purpurascens. Sharpest, most striking in-colour specimen.
Debra Baker; 1203x922. Salmon-pink adult, full side view over reef rubble showing the oblique-lined body pattern and eye detail; correct ID, good clarity. Non-recommended supporting shot.
Debra Baker; 930x766. Deeper-red colour phase in a dynamic head-down pose against turquoise reef, adds variety of colour and posture; slightly softer/more distant, kept as extra.

summary: The McCullochi Dottyback is a small, colorful reef fish from the Coral Sea and Western Pacific, generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly saltwater species. It tends to be semi-aggressive toward other dottybacks and ornamental shrimp but is broadly regarded as reef-safe with corals and sessile invertebrates.
careNotes:
The McCullochi Dottyback is generally considered one of the more straightforward saltwater fish to keep, taking meaty foods readily and adapting quickly to captivity once it locates a defended cave or crevice in the rockwork. It tends to be cryptic at first, then begins patrolling mid-water near its territory once settled.

A secure lid is generally advised — dottybacks as a group are known jumpers, and a well-fitted cover is broadly recommended for any pseudochromid setup. Providing ample live rock with caves, overhangs, and crevices gives the fish both shelter and areas for supplemental grazing.

Temperament is consistently described as semi-aggressive: only one specimen — or a bonded pair — is generally advised per aquarium, and other dottybacks or fish of similar shape and color should be avoided. The species has been reported to attack ornamental shrimp and may bully smaller or more docile tankmates; otherwise it is broadly regarded as reef-safe with corals and most sessile invertebrates.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is generally considered challenging in home aquariums, in part due to strong intraspecific aggression typical of the family. Like most pseudochromids, the species is believed to be a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite — the largest female has been reported to undergo a sex change if the dominant male is lost. Pairs are typically formed by introducing a smaller individual to a larger established one.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for care level (Easy), temperament (semi-aggressive), diet (carnivore — meaty foods), reef-compatible (yes), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), 30 US gal minimum tank baseline, 4-inch adult size, Coral Sea native range, and pair-tank guidance (≥100 gal).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for accepted scientific name Cypho purpurascens (De Vis, 1884), family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria, max length 7.5 cm TL, depth range 5–40 m, native distribution (Great Barrier Reef, Papua New Guinea, Tonga), reef-associated marine tropical habitat, and IUCN Least Concern (2015).

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24–27°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), and confirmed protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphroditism with harem formation. Reported adult size of 15 cm and 'peaceful' temperament were treated as outliers against FishBase/LiveAquaria/BlueZoo consensus and were not adopted; native-range claim (Red Sea to Line Islands) was also rejected in favor of FishBase.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked 30 US gal minimum, beginner care level, semi-aggressive temperament, carnivorous diet, reef-safe yes, and Coral Sea native range. Reported max size of 3 inches sat at the lower end of the size spread.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Genus/family-level care guide used for jumping/sealed-lid recommendation, conspecific-aggression advice (one dottyback per aquarium), and 20–30 US gal general minimum range applicable to small pseudochromids.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria is generally considered difficult, largely due to the strong intraspecific aggression characteristic of pseudochromids. The species is believed to be a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite that forms harems in the wild, with the dominant female reportedly capable of undergoing a sex change if the male is lost. Pairs in captivity are typically formed by introducing a smaller individual to a larger established one.
